channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘The Great Divide’ Review: Noah Kahan Is Caught in the In-Between
item.description: The singer-songwriter’s “Stick Season” was an unexpected smash. On its follow-up he sings about fame, complex relationships and the future with a sharper perspective.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Noah Kahan performing in February. His new album, “The Great Divide,” teems with the inner dialogues of an anxious mind.

item.title: They Were Michael Jackson’s ‘Second Family.’ Now They Say He Abused Them.
item.description: The Cascio siblings are suing Mr. Jackson’s estate after standing by him for years as he faced accusations of child molestation.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: The Cascio siblings: from left, Dominic, Aldo, Marie Nicole and Eddie.

item.title: Saudis Withdraw Offer of Millions to Metropolitan Opera
item.description: The arrangement would have brought up to $200 million to the Metropolitan Opera, which has suffered a series of financial setbacks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:description: Opening night at the Metropolitan Opera in September.

item.title: Is 1990s Alternative Rock the New Country?
item.description: Stagecoach Festival started out as the “country Coachella,” but has been morphing into a new home for ’90s rock bands slinging angst and guitar music.
item.dc:creator: David Malitz
item.media:description: Counting Crows in 1994. The band, led by Adam Duritz (bottom center), will perform at Stagecoach in California this weekend alongside other ’90s mainstays and a host of country acts.

item.title: Wayne Moss, Guitarist Who Helped Broaden Country Sound, Dies at 88
item.description: A top Nashville musician, he played on Bob Dylan’s “I Want You,” Roy Orbison’s “Oh, Pretty Woman” and Dolly Parton’s “Jolene.”
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren

item.title: Michael Harrison, 67, Dies; Inventive Composer Who Played With Tuning
item.description: Influenced by Indian raga and nontraditional tuning systems, he created hypnotic works with a spiritual bent and created an unconventional piano.
item.dc:creator: William Robin
item.media:description: Michael Harrison performing in 2001. “Revelation,” a spellbinding and lyrical structured improvisation built from reverberant overtones, was said by a fellow composer to have been his “magnum opus.”

item.title: Review: Barbara Hannigan Is Conductor, Director and Star
item.description: The soprano Barbara Hannigan, who also directed, is multitasking at the New York Philharmonic in a disturbingly resonant take on “La Voix Humaine.”
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Ferrari

item.title: Who’s Who in ‘Michael’: A Guide to the Characters
item.description: A new Michael Jackson biopic, with the star’s nephew Jaafar Jackson in the title role, is packed with famous musical figures. These are some of them.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Review: ‘The Rocky Horror Show’ Revival Gives Us What We Want
item.description: Sam Pinkleton’s new revival at Studio 54 gives us the big gay mayhem we want while also maintaining some order via Rachel Dratch’s droll Narrator.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich
item.media:description: Josh Rivera, center left, as Rocky, linking arms with Luke Evans as Frank-N-Furter in “The Rocky Horror Show” at Studio 54 in Manhattan.

item.title: Ruth Slenczynska, 101, Dies; Piano Prodigy Overcame Father’s Abuse
item.description: Treated brutally, she went from celebrated child star to 15-year-old “burned-out candle.” Against the odds, she later resumed performing.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:credit: James J. Kriegsmann, via Bettmann Archive/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ruth Slenczynska in 1957. Her story has been cited as a textbook example of the toxic consequences that can result when a controlling parent and a gifted child collide.

item.title: Michael Tilson Thomas, Celebrated American Conductor, Dies at 81
item.description: A galvanizing force in classical music as a conductor, composer, pianist and evangelist, he spent 25 years as music director of the San Francisco Symphony.
item.dc:creator: Anthony Tommasini
item.media:credit: Hiroyuki Ito for The New York Times
item.media:description: Experimentation and ambitious projects marked Mr. Thomas’s years at the San Francisco Symphony. He conducted the orchestra in a Stravinsky program at Carnegie Hall in 2018.

item.title: Controversies and Production Issues Around ‘Michael,’ the Jackson Biopic, Explained
item.description: Reshoots, reluctant studios and family holdouts: the production faced many issues. But now the box office is expected to be huge.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: Jaafar Jackson as Michael Jackson, his uncle, in the new biopic “Michael.”

item.title: ‘Beaches’ Review: A Classic Weepie Dries Its Tears
item.description: A new musical version of the 1980s tear-jerker comes to Broadway, but the production is too muddled to make an emotional impact.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kelli Barrett, left, and Jessica Vosk as the besties Bertie and Cee Cee in “Beaches” at the Majestic Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Alan Osmond, Who Led His Brothers in Song, Dies at 76
item.description: He was the eldest original member of the Osmonds, a family singing group that hit it big in the 1970s with songs like “One Bad Apple.”
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Alan Osmond onstage with his brothers in 1973. They began performing together in the 1950s and hit their peak in the early ’70s.

item.title: Judge Dismisses Sean ‘Diddy’ Combs’s $100 Million Defamation Suit Against NBC
item.description: The music mogul, now imprisoned, had sued after NBCUniversal’s streaming service, Peacock aired the documentary “Diddy: The Making of a Bad Boy” last year.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Sean Combs said an NBCUniversal documentary had defamed him, but a judge in New York ruled that his reputation had already been damaged by his criminal indictment and other matters.

item.title: D4vd Murder Case: Celeste Rivas Hernandez’s Cause of Death Is Revealed
item.description: The Los Angeles police sought to keep the findings secret for months as they investigated the singer D4vd, who was charged this week with the murder of the teenager.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: The severely decomposed remains of Celeste Rivas Hernandez were found in September, a day after what would have been her 15th birthday.

item.title: Review: At the Met, a Polished and Polite ‘Onegin’ Returns
item.description: A strong cast led by the soprano Asmik Grigorian lifts Deborah Warner’s production, which never quite convinces.
item.dc:creator: Arya Roshanian

item.title: Vilma Jää’s Voice Is Unlike Anything in Opera
item.description: Vilma Jää, a Finnish folk and pop singer, is bringing her eerie and affecting traditional music to the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and George Etheredge

item.title: Dave Mason, Songwriter and a Co-Founder of Traffic, Dies at 79
item.description: An English singer and guitarist, he wrote classic-rock mainstays like “Feelin’ Alright?” and “Only You Know and I Know.” He also had a successful solo career, singing “We Just Disagree.”
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.media:description: Dave Mason performing in 1980. As a solo artist, he released three gold albums, including his solo debut in 1970, “Alone Together.”

item.title: 7 New Songs You Should Hear Now
item.description: Nine Inch Nails’ LP with Boys Noize, Raye’s collaboration with Al Green, the latest from a Black Midi member and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Nine Inch Noize onstage at Coachella last weekend.

item.title: Nate Smith Is Bringing a Big-Tent Approach to the Newport Jazz Festival
item.description: The Grammy-winning drummer — and the event’s latest artistic director — uses new-school methods to maintain the genre’s essential tradition: live collaboration.
item.dc:creator: Elena Bergeron
item.media:credit: Riley Goodman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nate Smith said one of his goals as artistic director of the Newport Jazz Festival is to foster personal connections among musicians and listeners.

item.title: Gregory Spears: Composer Who Loops Musical Time and History
item.description: Gregory Spears is bringing his uncanny sense of genre and eras of music to the “Sleeping Beauty” story with his new opera, “Sleepers Awake.”
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Michelle Gustafson
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: The composer Gregory Spears in Philadelphia.

item.title: Madonna Says Her Vintage Outfit Is Missing After Coachella Performance
item.description: The pop star said she was “hoping and praying” for someone to find the ensemble, from her personal collection, after her surprise performance at the music festival.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.category: Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival
item.media:description: Madonna, right, returned to Coachella for the first time in 20 years, performing with Sabrina Carpenter, and brought some old clothing with her.

item.title: How John Dowland Built a Music Career on Tearful Melancholy
item.description: Dowland, who died 400 years ago, spun out sad songs that were popular in his time and continue to influence artists today.
item.dc:creator: Hugh Morris

item.title: ‘Schmigadoon!’ Review: Oh, What a Beautiful Sendup
item.description: Cinco Paul’s loving spoof of Golden Age musicals, adapted from a TV series, comes to Broadway, where its charming musical numbers can really shine.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli and Sara Krulwich

item.title: With Orban Out, the Pianist Andras Schiff Plans a Return to Hungary
item.description: Schiff has said he won’t perform in countries with what he calls strongmen leaders, a list that still includes the United States and Russia.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Andras Schiff, here at Carnegie Hall in 2019, said he was stunned and gratified by Prime Minister Viktor Orban’s defeat.

item.title: How Coachella Became a Pop Spectacle With Surprise Guests Like Madonna and Olivia Rodrigo
item.description: What started in the late ’90s as a rock and dance-music festival has evolved into a platform where social media, spectacle and unexpected guests make the headlines.
item.dc:creator: Caryn Ganz
item.category: Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival
item.media:description: Sabrina Carpenter, left, invited Madonna to perform in her headlining set during Coachella’s second weekend.

item.title: Singer D4vd Charged With Murder of Celeste Rivas Hernandez
item.description: The singer is accused of sexually abusing Celeste Rivas Hernandez before killing the teenager with a sharp weapon and mutilating her body. He has pleaded not guilty.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: The singer D4vd, 21, created the anthem for Fortnite. The severely decomposed remains of Celeste Rivas Hernandez were discovered in his car’s trunk in September.

item.title: Moya Brennan, Voice of the Gaelic Pop Band Clannad, Dies at 73
item.description: The group could be heard on the soundtracks of the movies “Patriot Games” and “The Last of the Mohicans.” Its “Theme From Harry’s Game,” from a TV series, was a hit.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub
item.media:description: Ms. Brennan in 2014 performing with the Gaelic pop band Clannad in London. She played the harp, but it was her voice — breathy, with a delicate vibrato — that provided the band’s signature sound.

item.title: Gwendolyn Chisolm, Who Rhymed on Rap’s First Female Hit, Dies at 66
item.description: A chance encounter led to overnight success for the Sequence, a seminal trio whose “Funk You Up” broke barriers.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: The Sequence in 1980. From left: Cheryl Cook (Cheryl the Pearl), Gwendolyn Chisolm (Blondy) and Angela Brown, later Angie Stone (Angie B.).

item.title: Don Schlitz, Prolific Writer of Country Music Hits, Dies at 73
item.description: He won a Grammy for the Kenny Rogers song “The Gambler,” and also wrote for Randy Travis, the Judds and Mary Chapin Carpenter.
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren

item.title: A Dance Party for Martha Graham With Ariana DeBose and Padma Lakshmi
item.description: Stars and luminaries honored the dance company’s 100th anniversary with dinner, performances and a champagne toast at the New York Public Library.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Rebecca Smeyne
item.category: Graham, Martha, Center of Contemporary Dance
New York Public Library for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times

item.title: After D4vd Arrest, Lake Elsinore Residents Mourn Celeste Rivas Hernandez
item.description: Celeste Rivas Hernandez went missing from Lake Elsinore, Calif., at age 13. The musician known as D4vd has been detained in connection with her death.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín and Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times

item.title: Luca Guadagnino Wants a Difficult Opera to Break Free of Polemics
item.description: The Italian filmmaker hopes his staging of John Adams’s “The Death of Klinghoffer” will help audiences look past the controversies surrounding the work.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:credit: Clara Vannucci for The New York Times
item.media:description: “For me, what matters is that — from a musical standpoint, from the perspective of the libretto — this is a major work,” Luca Guadagnino said.

item.title: A Chicago Man’s 10,000 Concerts on Tape Are Becoming Digital History
item.description: From the mid-1980s until just a few years ago, Aadam Jacobs recorded thousands of club shows, including an early Nirvana performance. Volunteers are adding them to an online archive.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:description: Aadam Jacobs at home in Chicago. The Chicago Tribune called him, among other things, “a tireless audio documentarian” for recording thousands of intimate concerts in his hometown.

item.title: Lana Del Rey, Prince: 10 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Lana Del Rey reveals a dramatic theme for a James Bond video game, and a look back at the chart from 40 years ago, when Prince reigned supreme.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Lana Del Rey’s new “First Light” provides the theme for the Bond video game “007 First Light.”
